description: PR introduces a security regression and an architecture smell. Two panelists return blocking-severity findings; CEO recommends needs_rework but frames it as advisory.

panelists.summary: Refactor splits a clean module into a circular import; same logic now lives in two places.

findings.summary: Circular import between resolver.py and downloader.py
findings.rationale: The new factory in resolver.py imports downloader.GithubDownloader at module top, while downloader.py imports resolver.create_resolver at module top. This works only because Python silently tolerates partial modules in sys.modules; the first import that fails will cascade across the whole install path.
findings.suggestion: Move the factory to a third module (deps/factory.py) that both depend on, or defer the import inside the function body.

findings.summary: validate_url duplicated across resolver.py and downloader.py
findings.rationale: Two copies that already disagree on trailing-slash handling. R3 EXTRACT trigger fired (>=3 call sites in the diff).

diagrams.component:
flowchart TD
    A[install pipeline] --> B[resolver.create_resolver]
    B --> C[downloader.GithubDownloader]
    C -->|imports at module top| B
    B -->|imports at module top| C

panelists.summary: Two new error strings ship outside STATUS_SYMBOLS; one new emoji slipped in.

findings.summary: Emoji character in error message will crash on Windows cp1252 terminals
findings.rationale: encoding.instructions.md is unambiguous: ASCII-only U+0020-U+007E. The rocket character on line 211 will raise UnicodeEncodeError under charmap.
Direct print() loses the panel's colorization and TTY-detection. Inconsistent with the other 38 call sites in this module.

panelists.summary: New error message is technically accurate but unhelpful to a user encountering it cold.

findings.summary: Error message lacks an actionable next step
findings.rationale: 'Invalid dependency reference' tells the user WHAT failed but not WHY or what to do. Compare to the message in install/pipeline.py:147 which suggests three remediations.
findings.suggestion: Suffix with 'Run `apm install --verbose` to see the resolved URL, or check `apm.yml` for typos.'

panelists.summary: Path-traversal regression: new code joins user-controlled segments without validate_path_segments.

findings.summary: User-controlled `dep.name` is path-joined without traversal validation
findings.rationale: path_security.instructions.md mandates validate_path_segments() at parse time for any user-provided value used in path construction. The new code at line 156 calls Path(install_dir) / dep.name directly. A malicious manifest with `name: ../../../etc/passwd` would traverse out of the install dir.
findings.suggestion: Wrap with `validate_path_segments(dep.name, context='dep.name')` before the join, then `ensure_path_within(result, install_dir)` after.

findings.summary: New URL probe uses raw requests.get without timeout
findings.rationale: Default no-timeout means a hostile or hung server can stall the install pipeline indefinitely.
findings.suggestion: Add timeout=30 (matches the convention in github_downloader.py:412).

panelists.summary: No README/CHANGELOG impact; nothing to amplify or warn about externally.
Diff does not touch token resolution, AuthResolver, HostInfo, or credential paths.
panelists.inactive_reason: PR touches only deps/resolver.py and deps/downloader.py refactor; no AuthResolver, HostInfo, or token-handling code in scope.

panelists.summary: Drift: behavior change but docs/src/content/docs/reference/dependencies.md still describes the pre-refactor flow.

findings.rationale: The doc says 'resolution is performed by GithubDownloader directly' but the refactor introduces a separate Resolver. Documentation drift will mislead first-time readers.
findings.suggestion: Update the resolution-flow section to name the new Resolver, or add a note that downloader-direct resolution is being phased out.

panelists.summary: Path-traversal regression has no regression-trap test; the malicious-name case is the test that would have caught this slip.

findings.summary: No test exercises a malicious dep.name (path-traversal payload) against the new resolver join
findings.rationale: The path-traversal regression at resolver.py:156 is exactly the surface that validate_path_segments + ensure_path_within exist to defend. A test that constructs a Dep with `name='../../../etc/passwd'` and asserts the resolver raises before joining is the regression-trap that prevents this from re-shipping. Absence of such a test in tests/unit/deps/ confirmed by `grep -rn 'validate_path_segments\|path_traversal' tests/unit/deps/` returning no match.
findings.suggestion: Add a parametrized test with traversal payloads ('../', '..\\', '/etc/passwd', '..%2f..') and assert each raises ValueError before any filesystem operation.

evidence.proves: User-controlled dep.name cannot escape the install directory via traversal payloads.

findings.summary: Refactor changes resolver/downloader integration but no integration test covers the cross-module flow
findings.rationale: Existing tests cover resolver.py and downloader.py in isolation; no test exercises the full install path end-to-end through both modules. A refactor that splits responsibilities across a module boundary needs at least one integration test that proves the boundary works.
findings.suggestion: Add an integration test that installs a real (test-fixture) dependency and asserts the file ends up in the expected location after going through both Resolver and Downloader.

evidence.proves: The new Resolver -> Downloader boundary actually delivers files to disk for a real apm.yml.

ceo.headline: Refactor direction is sound, but two correctness regressions (path traversal, Windows encoding) need to land before this can ship.
ceo.arbitration:
The architectural intent -- separating dependency resolution from download orchestration -- is the right call (Python Architect previously flagged the conflation as tech debt). However, this round introduces three regressions worth flagging before the next push:

The path-traversal slip at line 156 is the most important. `dep.name` is user-controlled via apm.yml, and the codebase has a strict invariant (path_security.instructions.md) that any path construction from user input MUST go through `validate_path_segments` + `ensure_path_within`. This is not a style nit -- it's the exact attack surface the centralized helpers exist to prevent.

The Windows-encoding regression is mechanically easy to fix (one character) but signals that the encoding rule is not yet automated in CI. Worth a follow-up to lint for non-ASCII bytes in source files.

The circular import is a real correctness risk Python tolerates only by accident; defer-import or extract-to-third-module both work.

Everything else (validate_url duplication, error-message helpfulness, doc drift, request timeout) is recommended-severity and can land in this PR or a follow-up.
ceo.dissent_notes: Python Architect and Supply Chain Security weighted the circular import as recommended vs blocking respectively; CEO sided with recommended because Python's partial-module tolerance has been stable for a decade and the import path is exercised by every install run.

principle_alignment.secure_by_default: The path-traversal regression is the exact failure mode `secure by default` is meant to prevent.

recommended_followups.summary: Add validate_path_segments + ensure_path_within around the dep.name join at resolver.py:156
recommended_followups.why: User-controlled path component without traversal validation; the codebase has a hard rule.

recommended_followups.summary: Add a regression-trap test exercising malicious dep.name (path-traversal payloads) against the resolver join
recommended_followups.why: The path-traversal slip is exactly the surface a regression-trap test would have caught; lock the contract in so this never re-ships.

recommended_followups.summary: Replace the rocket emoji at resolver.py:211 with `[!]` per STATUS_SYMBOLS
recommended_followups.why: Will crash on Windows cp1252 terminals.

recommended_followups.summary: Break the resolver.py <-> downloader.py circular import (factory module or deferred import)
recommended_followups.why: Tolerated by Python today but fragile to any change in import order.

recommended_followups.summary: Update docs/reference/dependencies.md to name the new Resolver
recommended_followups.why: Docs still describe the pre-refactor flow; drift will mislead first-time readers.

ship_recommendation.prose: Address the two blocking-severity items (path-traversal validation and Windows-encoding fix) before re-requesting review. The circular import is fragile but does not gate; resolve it in this PR if convenient, otherwise track as a follow-up. The remaining recommended items can land in this PR or in a series of small follow-ups -- maintainer's call.
